# AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/FlatBaseChange.lean

## Summary (iter-003, FBC-A lane)

Decomposed the monolithic crux sorry in `pushforward_base_change_mate_cancelBaseChange` into the
blueprint's `\uses`-linked chain and proved leaves-first. **L1, L2, L3 fully proved and
axiom-clean**; the parent mate lemma is now **proved modulo a single isolated residue (L4)**; the
crux is reduced from "an abstract sheaf-map's Γ is iso" to "a concrete tensor map is iso".

Result, file compiles `lake env lean` exit 0. Net sorry count unchanged (3) but structure vastly
improved: the old monolithic mate-lemma sorry is GONE (now a proved assembly), replaced by the
much smaller, concrete L4 generator-trace residue. The other two sorries (affine reduction @920,
flat @960) are pre-existing and downstream / deferred per PROGRESS.

## pullback_fst_snd_specMap_tensor (L1, ~line 707) — RESOLVED, axiom-clean
- **Approach:** State the leg identification in the `e.inv ≫ pullback.fst/snd = Spec.map (ofHom incl)`
  orientation under `letI := φ.hom.toAlgebra`, `letI := ψ.hom.toAlgebra`; close by Mathlib's
  `pullbackSpecIso_inv_fst`/`pullbackSpecIso_inv_snd`.
- **Key insight:** With the `φ`-algebra structure, `Spec.map φ` is *defeq* to
  `Spec.map (CommRingCat.ofHom (algebraMap ↑R ↑A))`, so `pullbackSpecIso`'s pullback over the
  `algebraMap` cospan IS the same `pullback.fst (Spec.map φ) (Spec.map ψ)`. No bridging lemma
  needed — `exact pullbackSpecIso_inv_fst ↑R ↑A ↑R'` closes it. Mathlib tensor order is
  `↑A ⊗[↑R] ↑R'` (S=A first, T=R'); blueprint's `R'⊗_R A` is the swap — I use Mathlib's order
  throughout (the def is project-local, not protected).

## base_change_mate_domain_read (L2, ~line 740) — RESOLVED, axiom-clean
- **Approach:** `Θ_src` as a composite of `Functor.mapIso`'d dictionaries + tilde–Γ unit:
  `moduleSpecΓFunctor.mapIso ((pullback (Spec.map ψ)).mapIso (pushforward_spec_tilde_iso φ M) ≪≫
  pullback_spec_tilde_iso ψ (restrictScalars φ.hom M)) ≪≫ (tilde.toTildeΓNatIso.app _).symm`.
- Target `(extendScalars ψ.hom).obj ((restrictScalars φ.hom).obj M)` = `R' ⊗_R M`. Cospan maps are
  genuine `Spec`-maps, so the dictionaries apply directly. Clean.

## base_change_mate_codomain_read (L3, ~line 770) — RESOLVED, axiom-clean
- **Approach:** Read `g' = pullback.fst`, `f' = pullback.snd` through `e := pullbackSpecIso`. Using
  L1, `pullback.fst = e.hom ≫ Spec.map inclA` (via `Iso.inv_comp_eq`), likewise snd. Then
  `pullbackCongr` + `pullbackComp` to factor `pullback g'` as `pullback (Spec.map inclA) ⋙
  pullback e.hom`, and `pushforwardCongr` + `pushforwardComp` for `pushforward f'`. The middle
  `(pushforward e.hom).obj ((pullback e.hom).obj W₀) ≅ W₀` is the adjunction-unit iso (see helper
  below). Finish with the two `Spec`-map dictionaries + tilde–Γ unit.
- Target `(restrictScalars includeRight.toRingHom).obj ((extendScalars includeLeftRingHom).obj M)`
  = `(A ⊗_R R') ⊗_A M` as `R'`-module (Mathlib tensor order).
- **Key insight / NEW HELPER (needs blueprint entry):** `pullbackIsoEquivalenceOfIso (f) [IsIso f]
  : Y.Modules ≌ X.Modules` and the instance `pullback_isEquivalence_of_iso : IsIso f →
  (pullback f).IsEquivalence`. Built by `Equivalence.mk (pullback f) (pullback (inv f))` with
  unit/counit assembled from `pullbackComp`/`pullbackCongr`/`pullbackId`; the coherence is
  discharged automatically by `Equivalence.mk`'s default `aesop_cat`. The instance makes
  `instIsIsoFunctorUnitOfIsEquivalence_1` fire, so `(asIso (pullbackPushforwardAdjunction e.hom).unit).app W₀`
  is the unit iso. This was the conceptual unlock for the whole leg-read.
- Note: `(asIso adj.unit).app W₀` (functor-iso `.app`) was required — `asIso (adj.unit.app W₀)`
  fails because the per-object `IsIso` instance does not auto-fire from `IsIso adj.unit`.

## base_change_mate_generator_trace (L4, line 830) — SORRY (isolated crux residue)
- **Statement (faithful, non-vacuous):** `IsIso (Θ_src⁻¹ ≫ Γ(α) ≫ Θ_tgt)` — the conjugated
  section map `R' ⊗_R M ⟶ (A ⊗_R R') ⊗_A M` is an isomorphism. This `IsIso` form mirrors the
  parent's formulation (per the blueprint NOTE iter-002 that the Lean decl records the `IsIso`
  corollary, not the literal equality). It is logically equivalent to the parent but the remaining
  obstacle is now a CONCRETE tensor map, not an abstract sheaf-map's Γ.
- **Obstacle (named):** the adjoint-mate generator trace. `pushforwardBaseChangeMap` is the
  `(g^*,g_*)`-transpose of `f_*(unit')`; tracing `r' ⊗ m` through the transpose + the two tilde
  dictionaries gives `r' ⊗ m ↦ (1 ⊗ r') ⊗ m` (Mathlib order), i.e. `cancelBaseChange⁻¹`.
- **cancelBaseChange orientation analysis (for the next agent):** Mathlib
  `TensorProduct.AlgebraTensorModule.cancelBaseChange R A B M' N : M' ⊗[A] (A ⊗[R] N) ≃ₗ[B] M' ⊗[R] N`.
  Our codomain is `(A ⊗_R R') ⊗_A M` (M on the RIGHT of `⊗_A`), which does NOT directly match the
  `M' ⊗_A (A⊗_R N)` shape. Route: `(A⊗_R R')⊗_A M ≃[comm_A] M ⊗_A (A⊗_R R') ≃[cancelBaseChange R A R' M R']
  M ⊗_R R' ≃[comm_R] R' ⊗_R M`. The comms must be the R'-LINEAR (heterobasic
  `AlgebraTensorModule.comm`) versions, since the conjugate is R'-linear with the R'-action coming
  through the `A⊗_R R'` factor. Constructing that regrouping equiv and proving the conjugate equals
  it on the generator `r'⊗m` is the residual multi-step build (blueprint effort 2011, flagged as a
  fine-break candidate). A `suffices conjugate = regroupEquiv.toModuleIso.hom` reduction would then
  close L4 via `rw; infer_instance`.
- `rw [ConcreteCategory.isIso_iff_bijective]` reduces the goal to `Function.Bijective ⇑(...)` but
  that is strictly harder than the IsIso-via-constructed-equiv route, so NOT taken.

## pushforward_base_change_mate_cancelBaseChange (parent, ~line 862) — PROVED modulo L4
- **Approach:** `set D := Θ_src, C := Θ_tgt, Γα := moduleSpecΓFunctor.map (pushforwardBaseChangeMap …)`;
  `haveI := base_change_mate_generator_trace`; then `Γα = D.hom ≫ (D.inv ≫ Γα ≫ C.hom) ≫ C.inv`
  (`simp [Category.assoc]`); `rw` + `infer_instance` closes `IsIso Γα` from the conjugate being iso
  (L4) and `D.hom`, `C.inv` isos. The old monolithic sorry + stale comment is REMOVED.

## affineBaseChange_pushforward_iso (line 920) — SORRY (pre-existing, NOT churned)
- Downstream of the mate lemma; the remaining content is the AFFINE REDUCTION (restrict an
  arbitrary cartesian square to the affine–affine model over each chart and identify
  `(pushforwardBaseChangeMap…).app U` with the affine–affine map). Blueprint flags this as a
  separate multi-hundred-LOC build (`lem:base_change_map_affine_local` handles only the locality
  criterion, already proved). Left with its existing partial + comment. Not blocked by L4.

## flatBaseChange_pushforward_isIso (line 960) — SORRY (deferred per PROGRESS, untouched)

## Needs blueprint entry
- `AlgebraicGeometry.pullbackIsoEquivalenceOfIso` (~line 745) — NEW helper. `(f : X ⟶ Y) [IsIso f]
  → Y.Modules ≌ X.Modules`. Uses: `Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp`, `pullbackCongr`, `pullbackId`,
  `IsIso.inv_hom_id`/`hom_inv_id`. Reviewer/planner: add `\label` + `\lean` + `\uses` so the
  1-to-1 Lean↔blueprint correspondence holds.
- `AlgebraicGeometry.pullback_isEquivalence_of_iso` (~line 759) — NEW instance.
  `(f : X ⟶ Y) [IsIso f] → (Scheme.Modules.pullback f).IsEquivalence`, via
  `(pullbackIsoEquivalenceOfIso f).isEquivalence_functor`. Uses: the def above +
  `Equivalence.isEquivalence_functor`. Needs a blueprint block.

## Blueprint marker flags (for review agent — I cannot edit blueprint)
- `lem:pullback_fst_snd_specMap_tensor`, `lem:base_change_mate_domain_read`,
  `lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read` are now fully proved/axiom-clean → ready for `\leanok` on
  both statement and proof (the deterministic `sync_leanok` should pick these up).
- `lem:base_change_mate_generator_trace` and `lem:pushforward_base_change_mate_cancelBaseChange`
  remain sorry-bearing (L4 residue) → statement `\leanok`, proof NOT.
</content>
</invoke>

Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | read | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
112	
113	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
114	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
115	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.
116	
117	## Agent Roles
118	
119	### Plan Agent
120	
121	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
122	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
123	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
124	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
125	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
126	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
127	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
128	
129	### Prover Agent
130	
131	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
132	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
133	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
134	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
135	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
136	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
137	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
138	
139	### Subagents
140	
141	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
142	
143	### Review Agent
144	
145	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
146	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
147	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
148	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
149	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
150	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
151	
152	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
153	
154	These run automatically between phases:
155	
156	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
157	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
158	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.
159

1	# Prover — Prover Stage
2	
3	You fill `sorry` placeholders with complete proofs in your assigned `.lean` file.
4	
5	## Workflow
6	
7	1. Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
8	2. Read `task_pending.md` for prior attempts, dead ends, relevant lemmas on your file.
9	3. Check the `.lean` file for `/- USER: ... -/` comments (file-specific user hints).
10	4. **Read the relevant blueprint chapter before writing Lean code.** The chapter holds the mathematical proof sketch you must align with. When stuck, re-reading the blueprint is often the fastest path forward.
11	5. Replace `sorry` with Lean proofs. Push as far as possible.
12	6. **Always save partial progress in the code.** If you can't fully close a sorry, leave your best attempt — commented-out steps, helper lemmas, partial `by` blocks with `sorry` at the stuck point. The file must still compile (use scoped `sorry` if needed), but your work must be visible for the next agent to continue from. NEVER revert to a bare `sorry` — that wastes the work.
13	7. Write your results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
14	
15	**Write permissions**: only your assigned `.lean` file(s) and `task_results/<your_file>.md`. Do NOT edit `PROGRESS.md`, `task_pending.md`, `task_done.md`, blueprint chapters, or other agents' files.
16	
17	## Know your target in the dependency graph (leandag)
18	
19	The blueprint dependency graph is queryable **read-only** — `archon` is on PATH, `--json` is parseable (the banner goes to stderr). Use it to ground your work instead of guessing:
20	
21	- `archon dag-query node --node <label> --json` — your target's blueprint entry, its `\lean{}` pin, and status. **If the `\lean{}` pin is a `…TODO.…` placeholder, the Lean declaration does not exist yet** — you must *create* it (the typed `sorry`, or the proof if your mode builds it), not "fill" a name that isn't in the environment.
22	- `archon dag-query ancestors --node <label> --json` — your target's full dependency closure. Build on deps that are actually done (`\leanok`); a dependency still marked ∞ / `sorry` is **not** a sound foundation — cite it only if it genuinely typechecks today.
23	- `archon dag-query gaps --json` — the ∞ holes (statements with no informal proof). If your target's cone hits one, the blueprint sketch is incomplete: flag it in your task_result rather than inventing a proof to paper over it.
24	- `archon dag-query unmatched --json` — Lean decls with no blueprint entry. After you add helpers, your new names surface here until they're blueprinted — that is the debt you must report (see Logging → "Needs blueprint entry").
25	
26	The label↔Lean mapping is the `\lean{}` annotation in the blueprint block; `leandag stats` / `leandag focus` also give a project-wide view. You never *write* the graph — that's the dag/plan/review agents' job — but reading it keeps you from proving against a hollow or unfinished foundation.
27	
28	## Protected declarations
29	
30	`archon-protected.yaml` lists declarations with **frozen signatures**. You may fill their proof bodies but must not rename, re-type, reorder arguments, or weaken hypotheses. Only the mathematician edits protected signatures.
31	
32	## Talking to the user (TO_USER.md)
33	
34	`.archon/TO_USER.md` is a *persistent* shared notice board surfaced to the user as a banner. You may add a bullet **only** for something the user genuinely must act on that you hit while proving — e.g. a missing credential/dependency a sorry is gated on, or a `/- USER: -/` question you cannot resolve. Discipline:
35	
36	- **Concise + relevant**: keep the whole file ≤ 2–3 short bullets; before adding, read it and delete any bullet no longer true. Never a question queue — the loop never waits.
37	- **Concurrency**: provers run in parallel (one per file). To avoid clobbering a sibling lane's bullet, append your single bullet prefixed with your file (`- [Foo/Bar.lean] …`) rather than rewriting the whole file, and only when it's truly user-actionable. Routine "couldn't close this sorry" notes go in `task_results/<your_file>.md`, NOT here.
38	
39	## Avoid early termination
40	
41	- Don't abandon a proof prematurely. Many complex proofs run to thousands of lines.
42	- Difficulty is NOT a valid reason to leave a `sorry`.
43	- Don't delegate to "the next iteration" or "another prover" if more effort could close it.
44	- Only modify the proof for your assigned task — leave unrelated proofs untouched.
45	- **Decompose**: break the proof into smaller sub-problems (following the blueprint's lemma structure when available: L1, L2, L3, …) and solve each individually until the entire goal closes.
46	
47	## Task completion criteria
48	
49	Your task is complete ONLY when ALL of:
50	
51	1. Every `sorry` in scope is replaced with a complete proof.
52	2. Zero axioms introduced.
53	3. The file compiles cleanly.
54	
55	## Never weaken the type to dodge the proof
56	
57	When the substantive type is unattainable this iter, you have one honest move: leave `sorry` with the **intended type signature** so the type itself encodes the claim. You must NOT substitute a tautologically-true type to trivialize the body. Three patterns are banned:
58	
59	- **Reflexive-iso placeholder.** Replacing a non-trivial `X ≅ Y` (or `Nonempty (X ≅ Y)`) target with `Nonempty (X ≅ X) := ⟨Iso.refl _⟩`. The type now asserts nothing; the docstring's "iter-N placeholder" disclosure does not undo the structural lie, and the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference will resolve to this hollow declaration.
60	- **`Classical.choice` body around an explicit witness.** Writing `Classical.choice (α := X) ⟨witness⟩` to dissolve `Type` vs `Prop` friction — the witness is unreachable through any unfold, so the def is indistinguishable from `Classical.choice ⟨any-other-X⟩` downstream. Either use `Classical.choose` on the existential chain (preserves the spec), or `Nonempty.some`-with-witness; or ship a typed `sorry`.
61	- **Empty-content `proof_wanted`.** `proof_wanted` discards the declaration post-elaboration — the named decl never enters the environment, so the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference breaks. Use `theorem name : T := by sorry` with the intended signature instead.
62	
63	**Litmus test**: if you `unfold` your declaration, does it expose the named substantive content (Kähler differential module, the explicit iso, …) or does it stop at `Classical.choice` / `Iso.refl _` / nothing? If the latter, the body is structurally vacuous — ship the typed `sorry` instead.
64	
65	## When infrastructure is missing
66	
67	Do NOT report "Mathlib lacks X" and stop. Before giving up:
68	
69	1. **Use the informal agent** (`.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py`): "Prove [goal] without using [missing infrastructure], only Mathlib." Even an imperfect sketch is valuable.
70	2. **Try the alternative** — formalize whatever the informal agent suggests.
71	3. **If you still can't**: write the alternative sketch to `informal/<theorem_name>.md` and record in your task result what you tried, why it failed, AND the alternative route you found. "I couldn't prove X, but here's approach Y that might work because Z" is far more useful than "infrastructure missing".
72	
73	When stuck more generally: break into smaller subgoals, search Mathlib more thoroughly, prove missing helpers yourself, try alternative strategies, re-read the blueprint, use Web Search for published proofs.
74	
75	**Impossibility vs difficulty**: technical difficulty → keep trying. Mathematical impossibility → immediately backtrack and document why.
76	
77	## Proof style
78	
79	- **Never modify working proofs** — if a declaration has no `sorry` and compiles, do not touch its body.
80	- Keep edits minimal; don't delete comments or change labels; don't add unrelated declarations.
81	- Helper lemmas you introduced may be modified if they turn out wrong.
82	- Add a concise comment above each helper lemma so reuse is easy.
83	- **List every new declaration you introduce under a `## Needs blueprint entry` heading in your task_result** (name, file, and the facts its proof relies on) — see Logging below. The project keeps a 1-to-1 Lean ↔ blueprint correspondence: the review agent and planner consume this list (and `archon dag-query unmatched`) to give each new declaration a blueprint block. An unreported helper is an invisible, isolated dependency that silently corrupts the frontier — flagging it is mandatory, not optional. (You never write the blueprint yourself; you only name what needs one.)
84	- **`change` vs `show`** — `change` reshapes the goal up to defeq; `show` is purely display-level annotation. Using `show` where `change` is needed produces a linter warning. Default to `change` when in doubt.
85	
86	## Mathlib tags in PROGRESS.md
87	
88	The plan agent tags suggested lemmas:
89	
90	- `[verified]` — confirmed to exist.
91	- `[expected]` — guessed by naming convention. Quick `lean_local_search`; pivot if it doesn't exist.
92	- `[gap]` — verified NOT in Mathlib. Don't waste search time; formalize a workaround.
93	
94	## LSP MCP tools
95	
96	The `archon-lean-lsp` server exposes Lean LSP operations as **MCP tool calls** (full names like `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_goal` / `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages`). The lean4 skill reference uses short names (`lean_goal`, `lean_local_search`, …) — same tools, NOT shell binaries.
97	
98	- Always invoke through your tool-call interface, the same way as `Read` or `Grep`.
99	- **Never** call `Bash` with `lean_goal …` etc. — there is no such shell command.
100	- First LSP action: `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages` on your file. If it returns `success: false` the server is cold; retry once or run `lake build` once via Bash, then retry.
101	
102	## Search protocol
103	
104	Follow `references/lean-lsp-tools-api.md`. Key priority:
105	
106	1. `lean_local_search` first.
107	2. `lean_leansearch` for semantic search — describe the mathematical content, not just the name.
108	3. `lean_loogle` for simple type patterns only.
109	4. Never use shell `find` / `grep` to locate Mathlib theorems.
110	
111	## Tooling traps
112	
113	- **Trust `goals_after`, not just empty diagnostics**: in `lean_multi_attempt`, `diagnostics: []` can be misleading (line-scoping quirks attach errors to the enclosing `by` block). Check that `goals_after` is empty or has advanced.
114	- **Beware `lean_run_code` with imports**: standalone snippets can silently swallow elaboration errors when they contain imports. Rely on the actual file's `lean_diagnostic_messages` for authoritative verification.
115	
116	## Logging
117	
118	Write to `task_results/<your_file>.md` (mirror your `.lean` path: `Algebra/WLocal.lean` → `task_results/Algebra_WLocal.lean.md`):
119	
120	```markdown
121	# Algebra/WLocal.lean
122	
123	## wLocal_iff (line 45)
124	### Attempt 1
125	- **Approach:** Direct case split on maximal ideals
126	- **Result:** FAILED — needed IsLocalRing instance, not available
127	- **Dead end:** direct case split without IsLocalRing
128	
129	### Attempt 2
130	- **Approach:** Stacks 0A31, characterize via bijection on spectra
131	- **Result:** RESOLVED
132	- **Key insight:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_injective` bridges the gap
133	
134	## helper_bijective (line 78)
135	### Attempt 1
136	- **Approach:** Split into injectivity + surjectivity; prove injectivity
137	- **Result:** PARTIAL (branch closed) — injectivity closed; surjectivity remains
138	- **Next step:** `PrimeSpectrum.range_comap_of_surjective` for the right branch
139	- **Lemmas found:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_surjective`

8	/-!
9	# Generic flatness of a coherent sheaf (A.2.a)
10	
11	This file packages the classical Grothendieck/Nitsure~\S4 **generic flatness**
12	theorem: over a noetherian integral base `S`, a coherent `𝓞_X`-module on a
13	finite-type morphism `X ⟶ S` becomes flat after restricting to a non-empty open
14	subscheme of `S`. This is the inductive engine underlying the
15	flattening-stratification construction of Grothendieck's existence theorem for
16	the Quot scheme.
17	
18	Each blueprint-pinned declaration carries the *intended* substantive type
19	signature (matching the `\lean{...}` pin in
20	`blueprint/src/chapters/Picard_FlatteningStratification.tex`) with a `sorry`
21	body where the proof is not yet supplied. The substantive generic-flatness
22	proof is deep (Nitsure~\S4: prime-filtration dévissage + Noether normalisation +
23	clearing denominators).
24	
25	The blueprint-pinned declaration is:
26	
27	- `AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatness` (theorem) — **generic flatness**
28	  [Nitsure §4 Theorem]: over a noetherian integral base `S`, a coherent sheaf
29	  on a finite-type `X ⟶ S` is flat over a non-empty open `V ⊆ S`. Its
30	  algebraic form (`thm:generic_flatness_algebraic`, no Lean pin) is the
31	  statement that a finite module over a finite-type algebra over a noetherian
32	  domain becomes free after inverting one non-zero element of the base.
33	
34	The file-internal `GenericFreeness` namespace lands the **finite-module /
35	finite-morphism special case** of the algebraic form axiom-clean, as a reusable
36	building block for the dévissage's leaves.
37	
38	## Note on type expressivity
39	
40	Following the project rule "Never weaken the type to dodge the proof",
41	`genericFlatness` requires the existence of a *non-empty* open `V` and
42	substantive flatness on every affine `U ⊆ V`; both quantifiers are necessary
43	(without them the statement collapses to `V = ∅`).
44	
45	## Mathlib status
46	
47	Mathlib (master `b80f227`) provides `Module.Flat`, `AlgebraicGeometry.Flat`
48	(morphism-level), `IsImmersion`, `IsLocallyNoetherian`, `IsIntegral`,
49	`LocallyOfFiniteType`, and the dévissage / Noether-normalisation infrastructure
50	the algebraic generic-flatness argument consumes. It does NOT yet provide the
51	polynomial-ring core of generic freeness, nor the geometric `genericFlatness`
52	statement packaging it over a noetherian integral base.
53	
54	## References
55	
56	Blueprint: `blueprint/src/chapters/Picard_FlatteningStratification.tex`.
57	Source: Nitsure, "Construction of Hilbert and Quot schemes", §4 (FGA Explained
58	Ch. 5, arXiv:math/0504020 pp. 5–18); Stacks Project tag 00HB (module flat).
59	-/

69	/-! ## Project-local Mathlib supplement — algebraic generic freeness (finite case)
70	
71	This section builds the **module-theoretic** generic-freeness statements that
72	underlie the geometric `genericFlatness` (blueprint
73	`thm:generic_flatness_algebraic`, Nitsure~\S4 "Lemma on Generic Flatness").
74	
75	The full algebraic statement — `A` a noetherian domain, `B` a *finite-type*
76	`A`-algebra, `M` a finite `B`-module ⟹ `∃ f ≠ 0` with `M_f` free over `A_f` —
77	is a deep theorem (prime-filtration dévissage + Noether normalisation +
78	clearing denominators). Mathlib already supplies most of the dévissage
79	machinery (`IsNoetherianRing.induction_on_isQuotientEquivQuotientPrime`,
80	`ModuleCat.free_shortExact`, `exists_finite_inj_algHom_of_fg`), but it does
81	**not** yet contain the polynomial-ring core (generic freeness for a finite
82	module over `A[X₁,…,X_d]`). See the file `task_results` handoff for the
83	precise decomposition of the remaining gap.
84	
85	What we *can* land axiom-clean here is the **finite-module / finite-morphism
86	case**: when `M` is finite as an `A`-module (in particular when `B` is
87	module-finite over `A`), generic freeness follows directly from
88	`Module.FinitePresentation.exists_free_localizedModule_powers` applied at the
89	generic point `Frac A`, where `M ⊗_A Frac A` is a finite vector space hence
90	free. This is a genuine special case of the algebraic generic-freeness
91	theorem (the case of a *finite* morphism `X → S`), and a reusable building
92	block for the dévissage's leaves. -/
93	
94	namespace GenericFreeness
95	
96	/-- **Generic freeness, finite-module case.** For a noetherian integral domain
97	`A` and a finite `A`-module `M`, there is a non-zero `f ∈ A` such that the
98	localisation `M_f` is free over `A_f = Localization.Away f`.
99	
100	This is the `d = 0` (finite-morphism) special case of the algebraic
101	generic-flatness theorem (`thm:generic_flatness_algebraic`, Nitsure~\S4):
102	inverting the generic point `Frac A`, the localised module is a finite vector
103	space hence free, and `Module.FinitePresentation.exists_free_localizedModule_powers`
104	descends that freeness to a single basic open `D(f) ⊆ Spec A`. -/
105	theorem exists_free_localizationAway_of_finite
106	    (A : Type*) (M : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A]
107	    [AddCommGroup M] [Module A M] [Module.Finite A M] :
108	    ∃ f : A, f ≠ 0 ∧
109	      Module.Free (Localization.Away f) (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers f) M) := by
110	  haveI : Module.FinitePresentation A M := Module.finitePresentation_of_finite A M
111	  obtain ⟨r, hr, hfree, _⟩ :=
112	    Module.FinitePresentation.exists_free_localizedModule_powers (nonZeroDivisors A)
113	      (LocalizedModule.mkLinearMap (nonZeroDivisors A) M) (FractionRing A)
114	  exact ⟨r, nonZeroDivisors.ne_zero hr, hfree⟩
115	
116	/-- **Generic flatness, finite-module case.** The flatness form of
117	`exists_free_localizationAway_of_finite`: for a noetherian domain `A` and a
118	finite `A`-module `M`, there is a non-zero `f` with `M_f` flat over `A_f`.
119	This is the affine-local content of `genericFlatness` for a finite morphism. -/
120	theorem exists_flat_localizationAway_of_finite
121	    (A : Type*) (M : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A]
122	    [AddCommGroup M] [Module A M] [Module.Finite A M] :
123	    ∃ f : A, f ≠ 0 ∧
124	      Module.Flat (Localization.Away f) (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers f) M) := by
125	  obtain ⟨f, hf, hfree⟩ := exists_free_localizationAway_of_finite A M
126	  haveI := hfree
127	  exact ⟨f, hf, Module.Flat.of_free⟩
128	
129	/-- **Generic freeness, finite-morphism case.** If `A` is a noetherian domain,
130	`B` a *module-finite* `A`-algebra, and `M` a finite `B`-module (with the
131	compatible `A`-module structure), then there is a non-zero `f ∈ A` with `M_f`
132	free over `A_f`. Reduces to `exists_free_localizationAway_of_finite` via
133	`Module.Finite.trans` (a finite module over a module-finite algebra is finite
134	over the base). This is generic flatness for a *finite* morphism `X → S`. -/
135	theorem exists_free_localizationAway_of_moduleFinite
136	    (A : Type*) (B : Type*) (M : Type*)
137	    [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A]
138	    [CommRing B] [Algebra A B] [Module.Finite A B]
139	    [AddCommGroup M] [Module B M] [Module.Finite B M]
140	    [Module A M] [IsScalarTower A B M] :
141	    ∃ f : A, f ≠ 0 ∧
142	      Module.Free (Localization.Away f) (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers f) M) := by
143	  haveI : Module.Finite A M := Module.Finite.trans B M
144	  exact exists_free_localizationAway_of_finite A M
145	
146	end GenericFreeness
147	
148	/-! ## Generic flatness, algebraic form (Nitsure §4 "Lemma on Generic Flatness")
149	
150	For a noetherian domain `A`, a finite-type `A`-algebra `B`, and a finite
151	`B`-module `M` (viewed as an `A`-module through the scalar tower `A → B → M`),
152	there is a non-zero `f ∈ A` such that `M_f` is free over `A_f`.
153	
154	The proof splits along the blueprint's decomposition:
155	
156	* **Primary route** (provided here, axiom-clean): when `M` is already
157	  *module-finite over `A`*, this is the finite-module helper
158	  `GenericFreeness.exists_free_localizationAway_of_finite` — over `Frac A` the
159	  localised module is a finite vector space hence free, and
160	  `Module.FinitePresentation.exists_free_localizedModule_powers` descends that
161	  freeness to a single basic open `D(f)`.
162	
163	* **Surviving residue** (`sorry` this iter): when `M` is finite over the
164	  *finite-type* algebra `B` but not module-finite over `A`, the genuine §4
165	  dévissage is required — a prime filtration of `M` as a finite `B`-module
166	  reduces to `M = B/𝔭`, Noether normalisation makes `B_g` finite over the
167	  polynomial ring `A_g[b₁,…,b_n]`, and induction on the support dimension
168	  bottoms out at the polynomial-ring core of generic freeness. That core
169	  (a finite module over `A[X₁,…,X_d]` is generically free) is the precise
170	  piece Mathlib does not yet supply. -/
171	
172	/-- **Generic flatness, algebraic form** (Nitsure §4 "Lemma on Generic
173	Flatness"). Let `A` be a noetherian domain, `B` a finite-type `A`-algebra, and
174	`M` a finite `B`-module regarded as an `A`-module via the scalar tower
175	`A → B → M`. Then there exists `f ∈ A`, `f ≠ 0`, such that
176	`LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers f) M` is free over `Localization.Away f`.
177	
178	Blueprint: `thm:generic_flatness_algebraic`. The finite-`A`-module case is the
179	thin Mathlib wrapper `GenericFreeness.exists_free_localizationAway_of_finite`;
180	the finite-type residue is the classical §4 dévissage (still `sorry`). -/
181	theorem genericFlatnessAlgebraic
182	    (A B M : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A]
183	    [CommRing B] [Algebra A B] [Algebra.FiniteType A B]
184	    [AddCommGroup M] [Module B M] [Module.Finite B M]
185	    [Module A M] [IsScalarTower A B M] :
186	    ∃ f : A, f ≠ 0 ∧
187	      Module.Free (Localization.Away f) (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers f) M) := by
188	  by_cases hAM : Module.Finite A M
189	  · -- Primary route: `M` module-finite over `A` ⟹ the finite-module helper.
190	    haveI := hAM
191	    exact GenericFreeness.exists_free_localizationAway_of_finite A M
192	  · -- Surviving residue: `M` finite over the finite-type algebra `B` but not
193	    -- module-finite over `A`. The Nitsure §4 dévissage (prime filtration +
194	    -- Noether normalisation + induction on support dimension) reduces this to
195	    -- the polynomial-ring core of generic freeness, which Mathlib does not yet
196	    -- provide. See `task_results` for the precise remaining gap.
197	    sorry
198	
199	/-! ## Generic flatness (Nitsure §4)
200	
201	Over a noetherian integral base `S`, a coherent sheaf on a finite-type
202	`X ⟶ S` is flat above some non-empty open `V ⊆ S`. This is the inductive
203	engine of the flattening-stratification theorem: combined with
204	Noetherian induction on the closed complement `S ∖ V`, it produces the
205	finite stratification of `S` by flatness loci.
206	
207	Algebraically (theorem `generic_flatness_algebraic`, no Lean pin): for a
208	noetherian domain `A`, a finite-type `A`-algebra `B`, and a finite
209	`B`-module `M`, there exists a non-zero `f ∈ A` such that `M_f` is a
210	free `A_f`-module. The geometric form (this declaration) restricts to a
211	non-empty affine open `Spec A ⊆ S` and applies the algebraic form on
212	each finite-type-algebra patch of `X` above `Spec A`.
213	
214	Blueprint reference: `thm:generic_flatness` (Nitsure §4). -/
215	
216	/-- **Generic flatness theorem** (Nitsure §4 / Stacks ?).
217	
218	For a noetherian integral scheme `S`, a finite-type morphism `p : X ⟶ S`,
219	and a coherent `𝓞_X`-module `𝓕`, there exists a non-empty open subscheme
220	`V ⊆ S` such that `𝓕|_{X_V} = 𝓕|_{p⁻¹V}` is flat over `𝓞_V`.
221	
222	iter-177+: the body follows Nitsure §4: pass to a non-empty affine open

1	\chapter{Flattening Stratification of a Coherent Sheaf}
2	\label{chap:Picard_FlatteningStratification}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FlatteningStratification.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE. This chapter packages the classical Grothendieck/Nitsure~\S4
6	% \emph{generic flatness} theorem: over a noetherian integral base, a coherent
7	% sheaf on a finite-type \(X \to S\) becomes flat over a non-empty open
8	% subscheme of \(S\). Both its algebraic form (generic freeness of a finite
9	% module over a finite-type algebra over a noetherian domain) and its
10	% geometric form are recorded here as the prover-ready specification for
11	% \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FlatteningStratification.lean}.
12	%
13	% This chapter is independently parallel-startable: it does not gate on the
14	% Route~A.1.a relative-Spec chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}.
15	
16	\section{Setup and motivation}
17	\label{sec:flatstrat_setup}
18	
19	Generic flatness is the foundational input to the flattening-stratification
20	construction underlying Grothendieck's existence theorem for the Quot scheme
21	(\cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}, \S4--\S5).  Its content is that flatness of a
22	coherent sheaf is a generic property over an integral base: over a noetherian
23	integral scheme \(S\), a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module on a finite-type morphism
24	\(X \to S\) becomes flat after restricting to a non-empty open subscheme of
25	\(S\). Algebraically this is the statement that a finite module over a
26	finite-type algebra over a noetherian domain becomes free after inverting a
27	single non-zero element of the base ring.
28	
29	We work throughout with a noetherian (integral, where required) base scheme
30	\(S\), a finite-type morphism \(p : X \to S\), and a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module
31	\(\F\).
32	
33	\section{Generic flatness}
34	\label{sec:generic_flatness}
35	
36	The construction of the flattening stratification proceeds by Noetherian
37	induction on \(S\). The induction step relies on the following classical
38	\emph{generic flatness} theorem of Grothendieck: over a noetherian integral
39	base, a coherent sheaf is flat over a non-empty open subscheme. Algebraically,
40	this is the assertion that a finitely generated module over a finite-type
41	algebra over a noetherian domain is generically free after inverting a single
42	non-zero element of the base ring.
43	
44	\begin{theorem}
45	
46	\leanok
47	[Generic flatness, algebraic form]
48	  \label{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic}
49	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatnessAlgebraic}
50	  \uses{lem:gf_torsion_base, lem:noeth_prime_filtration, lem:gf_splice_shortExact,
51	        lem:gf_noether_clear_denominators, lem:gf_polynomial_core, lem:gf_finite_module}
52	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness''
53	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1711-L1716).
54	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
55	  % "Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain,
56	  %  and \(B\) a finite type \(A\) algebra. Let \(M\) be a finite \(B\)-module.
57	  %  Then there exists an \(f\in A\), \(f\ne 0\), such that
58	  %  the localisation \(M_f\) is a free module over \(A_f\)."
59	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness''.}
60	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(B\) a finite-type \(A\)-algebra, and \(M\) a finite
61	  \(B\)-module. Then there exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that the localisation
62	  \(M_f\) is a free \(A_f\)-module.
63	
64	  The formal target \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatnessAlgebraic} regards \(M\) as an
65	  \(A\)-module through the scalar tower \(A \to B \to M\), and packages the
66	  localisation \(M_f\) as the localised module at the powers of \(f\): there exists
67	  \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that \texttt{LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers f) M}
68	  is free over \texttt{Localization.Away f}.
69	\end{theorem}
70	
71	% INTENDED LEAN SIGNATURE (the scaffold pass creates the decl pinned above with
72	% exactly this signature):
73	%   (A B M : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A]
74	%     [CommRing B] [Algebra A B] [Algebra.FiniteType A B]
75	%     [AddCommGroup M] [Module B M] [Module.Finite B M]
76	%     [Module A M] [IsScalarTower A B M] :
77	%     ∃ f : A, f ≠ 0 ∧
78	%       Module.Free (Localization.Away f) (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers f) M)
79	
80	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Over the quotient field \(K\) of \(A\), the \(K\)-algebra
81	% \(B_K = K\otimes _AB\) is of finite type, and \(M_K = K\otimes_AM\) is a
82	% finite module over \(B_K\). Let \(n\) be the dimension of the support
83	% of \(M_K\) over \(\spec B_K\). We argue by induction on \(n\), starting
84	% with \(n=-1\) which is the case when \(M_K =0\). [...]
85	% Now let \(n\ge 0\), and let the lemma be proved for smaller values.
86	% As \(B\) is noetherian and \(M\) is assumed to be a finite \(B\)-module,
87	% there exists a finite filtration
88	% \(0=M_0 \subset \ldots  \subset M_r =M\)
89	% where each \(M_i\) is a \(B\)-submodule of \(M\) such that for each \(i\ge 1\)
90	% the quotient module \(M_i/M_{i-1}\) is isomorphic to \(B/\pp_i\) for
91	% some prime ideal \(\pp_i\) in \(B\). [...] By Noether normalisation lemma,
92	% there exist elements \(b_1,\ldots,b_n \in B\), such that \(K\otimes_AB\) is
93	% finite over its subalgebra \(K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]\) [...]"
94	% (Source: nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1719-L1772; full
95	% proof omitted for length, structural steps restated below in project notation.)
96	\begin{proof}
97	
98	  \textbf{Primary route — the finite \(A\)-module case as a thin Mathlib wrapper.}
99	  Suppose first that \(M\) is module-finite over \(A\) itself (i.e.\ \(M\) is a finite
100	  \(A\)-module, not merely a finite \(B\)-module). Since \(A\) is noetherian, a finite
101	  \(A\)-module is finitely presented over \(A\). Let \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\) be the
102	  localisation of \(A\) at the multiplicative set \(S = A \setminus \{0\}\). The
103	  generic fibre \(M_K = K \otimes_A M = S^{-1} M\) is a \emph{finite-dimensional}
104	  \(K\)-vector space, hence free over \(K\); thus the freeness hypothesis at the
105	  generic localisation is automatic, with no input from the structure of \(B\). The
106	  result is then exactly Mathlib's
107	  \texttt{Module.FinitePresentation.exists\_free\_localizedModule\_powers}
108	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Localization.Free}): a finitely-presented \(A\)-module
109	  that is free over the localisation \(A_S = K\) becomes free over
110	  \texttt{Localization.Away}~\(r\) after inverting some \(r \in S\), i.e.\ some
111	  \(r \in A\), \(r \ne 0\). Setting \(f := r\) gives \(M_f\) free over \(A_f\). (The
112	  openness of the free locus is the \texttt{Module.freeLocus} API of
113	  \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Spectrum.Prime.FreeLocus}, which records that the locus
114	  where \(M\) is free over \(A\) is open and, by the above, non-empty.) This closes
115	  the theorem whenever \(M\) is finite over \(A\).
116	
117	  \textbf{Surviving residue — reducing the finite-type \(B\)-algebra case to the
118	  finite \(A\)-module case.} The genuine content not supplied by Mathlib is the
119	  reduction of the general hypothesis (\(M\) finite over a \emph{finite-type}
120	  \(A\)-algebra \(B\)) to the finite \(A\)-module situation handled above. Concretely,
121	  the missing piece is the polynomial-ring core of generic freeness: a finite module
122	  over \(A[b_1, \dots, b_n]\) becomes free over \(A_f\) after inverting a single
123	  \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\). The classical \S4 dévissage below is the fallback route
124	  for exactly this residue.
125	
126	  \emph{Fallback (Nitsure \S4 induction), as a \texttt{\textbackslash uses}-linked chain.}
127	  The \S4 dévissage is assembled from the sub-lemmas of
128	  \cref{sec:gf_devissage_chain}. If \(M_K = 0\), the torsion base case
129	  \cref{lem:gf_torsion_base} gives \(f \ne 0\) with \(M_f = 0\) free. Otherwise the
130	  prime-filtration induction principle \cref{lem:noeth_prime_filtration} over the
131	  noetherian ring \(B\), combined with the splicing fact
132	  \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact}, reduces to \(M = B/\mathfrak{p}\) and hence to
133	  \(B\) a domain, \(M = B\); Noether normalisation with clearing of denominators
134	  \cref{lem:gf_noether_clear_denominators} makes \(B_g\) finite over a polynomial
135	  ring \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\), and the polynomial-ring core
136	  \cref{lem:gf_polynomial_core} — bottoming out at the finite-\(A\)-module leaf
137	  \cref{lem:gf_finite_module} of the primary route — produces the required
138	  \(f \ne 0\) with \(M_f\) free over \(A_f\).
139	\end{proof}
140	
141	\subsection{Dévissage chain for the finite-type residue}
142	\label{sec:gf_devissage_chain}
143	
144	The following sub-lemmas decompose the surviving residue of
145	\cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic} — the case where \(M\) is finite over the
146	finite-type \(A\)-algebra \(B\) but not module-finite over \(A\) — into the
147	classical \cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}~\S4 induction. They are ordered so that each
148	\texttt{\textbackslash uses} only earlier blocks (or Mathlib anchors). Throughout,
149	\(A\) is a noetherian domain with fraction field \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\), \(B\) a
150	finite-type \(A\)-algebra, and \(M\) a finite \(B\)-module with the compatible
151	\(A\)-module structure via the scalar tower \(A \to B \to M\).
152	
153	\begin{lemma}[Finite-module leaf — finite \(A\)-module case]
154	  \label{lem:gf_finite_module}
155	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_free_localizationAway_of_finite}
156	  \uses{lem:fp_free_descent}
157	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain and \(M\) a finite \(A\)-module. Then there
158	  exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that \(M_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
159	\end{lemma}
160	
161	\begin{proof}
162	  This is the \(d = 0\) leaf of the induction and is already closed in Lean
163	  axiom-clean. A finite module over a noetherian ring is finitely presented, so
164	  \(M\) is finitely presented over \(A\). Over the generic point
165	  \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A) = S^{-1}A\) (\(S = A \setminus \{0\}\)) the localisation
166	  \(M_K = S^{-1}M\) is a finite-dimensional \(K\)-vector space, hence free over
167	  \(K\). The finite-presentation free-descent lemma \cref{lem:fp_free_descent}
168	  then descends this freeness to a single basic open: there is \(r \in S\), i.e.\
169	  \(r \in A\) with \(r \ne 0\), such that \(M_r\) is free over \(A_r\). Take
170	  \(f := r\).
171	\end{proof}
172	
173	\begin{lemma}[Finite-presentation free descent]
174	  \label{lem:fp_free_descent}
175	  \lean{Module.FinitePresentation.exists_free_localizedModule_powers}
176	  \mathlibok
177	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
178	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(S \subseteq R\) a multiplicative subset, and
179	  \(M\) a finitely-presented \(R\)-module such that the localisation \(M_S\) is
180	  free over the localisation \(R_S\). Then there exists \(r \in S\) such that
181	  \(M_r\) is free over \(R_r = \texttt{Localization.Away}~r\) (of the same finite
182	  rank).
183	\end{lemma}
184	
185	\begin{lemma}[L1 — torsion base case \(M_K = 0\)]
186	  \label{lem:gf_torsion_base}
187	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_free_localizationAway_of_torsion}
188	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, base case
189	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1719-L1726).
190	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
191	  % "We argue by induction on $n$, starting with $n=-1$ which is the case when
192	  %  $M_K =0$. In this case, as $K\otimes _AM = S^{-1}M$ where $S = A - \{ 0\}$,
193	  %  each $v\in M$ is annihilated by some non-zero element of $A$. Taking a finite
194	  %  generating set, and a common multiple of corresponding annihilating elements,
195	  %  we see there exists an $f\ne 0$ in $A$ with $fM=0$. Hence $M_f =0$, proving
196	  %  the lemma when $n=-1$."
197	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (base case of the
198	  induction).}
199	  Suppose \(K \otimes_A M = 0\), where \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\). Then there exists
200	  \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), with \(f M = 0\); consequently \(M_f = 0\) is free over
201	  \(A_f\).
202	\end{lemma}
203	
204	\begin{proof}
205	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1719-L1726).
206	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (base case).}
207	  Since \(K \otimes_A M = S^{-1}M\) with \(S = A \setminus \{0\}\), the hypothesis
208	  \(M_K = 0\) means every element of \(M\) is killed by some non-zero element of
209	  \(A\). Choose a finite \(B\)-generating set \(v_1, \dots, v_k\) of \(M\) (which
210	  exists because \(M\) is a finite \(B\)-module), and for each \(i\) an
211	  \(a_i \in A\), \(a_i \ne 0\), with \(a_i v_i = 0\). The product
212	  \(f := a_1 \cdots a_k\) is non-zero (\(A\) is a domain) and annihilates every
213	  generator, hence \(f M = 0\). Therefore \(M_f = 0\), which is free over \(A_f\).
214	\end{proof}
215	
216	\begin{lemma}[L2 — prime-filtration induction principle]
217	  \label{lem:noeth_prime_filtration}
218	  \lean{IsNoetherianRing.induction_on_isQuotientEquivQuotientPrime}
219	  \mathlibok
220	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
221	  Let \(B\) be a noetherian commutative ring and let \(P\) be a predicate on finite
222	  \(B\)-modules that (i) holds for every subsingleton module, (ii) holds for every
223	  cyclic quotient \(B/\mathfrak{p}\) with \(\mathfrak{p}\) prime, and (iii) is
224	  closed under short exact sequences (\(P(N_1)\) and \(P(N_3)\) imply \(P(N_2)\)
225	  for \(0 \to N_1 \to N_2 \to N_3 \to 0\)). Then \(P\) holds for every finite
226	  \(B\)-module. In particular every finite \(B\)-module \(M\) admits a finite
227	  filtration \(0 = M_0 \subset \cdots \subset M_r = M\) with each quotient
228	  \(M_i / M_{i-1} \cong B/\mathfrak{p}_i\) for a prime \(\mathfrak{p}_i\), so to
229	  prove generic freeness for \(M\) it suffices to prove it for each
230	  \(B/\mathfrak{p}\) and to splice along the filtration's short exact sequences.
231	\end{lemma}
232	
233	\begin{lemma}[L3 — splicing fact for a short exact sequence]
234	  \label{lem:gf_splice_shortExact}
235	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_free_localizationAway_of_shortExact}
236	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, splicing remark
237	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1737-L1745).
238	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
239	  % "Note that if $0\to M'\to M\to M'' \to 0$ is a short exact sequence of
240	  %  $B$-modules, and if $f'$ and $f''$ are non-zero elements of $A$ such that
241	  %  $M'_{f'}$ and $M''_{f''}$ are free over respectively $A_{f'}$ and $A_{f''}$,
242	  %  then $M_f$ is a free module over $A_f$ where $f=f'f''$. We will use this fact
243	  %  repeatedly. Therefore it is enough to prove the result when $M$ is of the form
244	  %  $B/\pp$ for a prime ideal $\pp$ in $B$. This reduces us to the case where $B$
245	  %  is a domain and $M=B$."
246	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (splicing fact).}
247	  Let \(0 \to M' \to M \to M'' \to 0\) be a short exact sequence of \(B\)-modules,
248	  and let \(f', f'' \in A\) be non-zero with \(M'_{f'}\) free over \(A_{f'}\) and
249	  \(M''_{f''}\) free over \(A_{f''}\). Then for \(f := f' f''\) the localisation
250	  \(M_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
251	\end{lemma}
252	
253	\begin{proof}
254	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1737-L1745).
255	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (splicing fact).}
256	  Localisation at the multiplicative set generated by \(f = f'f''\) is exact, so it
257	  carries the given short exact sequence to a short exact sequence
258	  \(0 \to M'_f \to M_f \to M''_f \to 0\) of \(A_f\)-modules. Both ends are free over
259	  \(A_f\): \(M'_f\) is the further localisation of the free \(A_{f'}\)-module
260	  \(M'_{f'}\) at \(f''\) and \(M''_f\) likewise, and a localisation of a free module
261	  is free. A short exact sequence with free quotient \(M''_f\) splits, so
262	  \(M_f \cong M'_f \oplus M''_f\) is free over \(A_f\) as a direct sum of free
263	  modules.
264	\end{proof}
265	
266	\begin{lemma}[L4 anchor — Noether normalisation over a field]
267	  \label{lem:noether_normalization_fg}
268	  \lean{exists_finite_inj_algHom_of_fg}
269	  \mathlibok
270	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
271	  Let \(k\) be a field and \(R\) a non-trivial finite-type \(k\)-algebra. Then there
272	  exist \(s \in \mathbb{N}\) and an injective \(k\)-algebra homomorphism
273	  \(k[X_1, \dots, X_s] \to R\) that is module-finite, i.e.\ \(R\) is a finite module
274	  over a polynomial subalgebra on algebraically independent generators.
275	\end{lemma}
276	
277	\begin{lemma}[L4 — Noether normalisation with clearing of denominators]
278	  \label{lem:gf_noether_clear_denominators}
279	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_localizationAway_finite_mvPolynomial}
280	  \uses{lem:noether_normalization_fg}
281	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, normalisation step
282	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1747-L1758).
283	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
284	  % "By Noether normalisation lemma, there exist elements $b_1,\ldots,b_n \in B$,
285	  %  such that $K\otimes_AB$ is finite over its subalgebra $K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$ and
286	  %  the elements $b_1,\ldots, b_n$ are algebraically independent over $K$. [...]
287	  %  If $g\ne 0$ in $A$ is chosen to be a `common denominator' for coefficients of
288	  %  equations of integral dependence satisfied by a finite set of algebra
289	  %  generators for $K\otimes_AB$ over $K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$, we see that $B_g$ is
290	  %  finite over $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$."
291	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (Noether
292	  normalisation with denominators).}
293	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain with fraction field \(K\) and \(B\) a finite-type
294	  \(A\)-algebra that is a domain with \(B_K = K \otimes_A B \ne 0\). Then there exist
295	  \(g \in A\), \(g \ne 0\), and elements \(b_1, \dots, b_n \in B\), algebraically
296	  independent over \(K\), such that the localisation \(B_g\) is module-finite over
297	  its polynomial subalgebra \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\).
298	\end{lemma}
299	
300	\begin{proof}
301	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1747-L1758).
302	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (Noether normalisation with denominators).}
303	  Apply Noether normalisation over the field \(K\)
304	  (\cref{lem:noether_normalization_fg}) to the finite-type \(K\)-algebra
305	  \(B_K = K \otimes_A B\): there are algebraically independent
306	  \(\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n \in B_K\) with \(B_K\) module-finite over
307	  \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\). Clearing denominators, each \(\bar b_j\) is
308	  represented by some \(b_j \in B\) (writing \(1 \otimes b\) as \(b\)). Fix a finite
309	  set of algebra generators of \(B_K\) over \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\) and
310	  finitely many equations of integral dependence they satisfy; let \(g \in A\),
311	  \(g \ne 0\), be a common denominator of the (finitely many) coefficients of those
312	  equations. After inverting \(g\) all these integrality relations hold over
313	  \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\), and the chosen generators generate \(B_g\) as a module
314	  over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\); hence \(B_g\) is module-finite over
315	  \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\).
316	\end{proof}
317	
318	\begin{lemma}[L5 — polynomial-ring core of generic freeness]
319	  \label{lem:gf_polynomial_core}
320	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_free_localizationAway_polynomial}
321	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module, lem:gf_splice_shortExact, lem:gf_torsion_base}
322	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, inductive step
323	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1760-L1772).
324	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
325	  % "Let $m$ be the generic rank of the finite module $B_g$ over the domain
326	  %  $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$. Then we have a short exact sequence of
327	  %  $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$-modules of the form
328	  %  $0\to A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]^{\oplus m} \to B_g \to T \to 0$ where $T$ is a finite
329	  %  torsion module over $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$. Therefore, the dimension of the
330	  %  support of $K\otimes_{A_g}T$ as a $K\otimes_{A_g}(B_g)$-module is strictly less
331	  %  than $n$. Hence by induction on $n$ (applied to the data $A_g$, $B_g$, $T$),
332	  %  there exists some $h\ne 0$ in $A$ with $T_h$ free over $A_{gh}$. Taking $f=gh$,
333	  %  the lemma follows from the above short exact sequence."
334	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (polynomial-ring
335	  core of the induction).}
336	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain and \(d \ge 0\). Let \(N\) be a finite module over
337	  the polynomial ring \(A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\), regarded as an \(A\)-module. Then there
338	  exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that \(N_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
339	
340	  This is the genuine Mathlib-absent residue of generic freeness: the bottom of the
341	  Nitsure \S4 induction, on which the whole finite-type case rests.
342	\end{lemma}
343	
344	\begin{proof}
345	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1760-L1772).
346	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (polynomial-ring core).}
347	  We induct on \(d\). For \(d = 0\) the ring is \(A\) itself and \(N\) is a finite
348	  \(A\)-module, so the claim is exactly the finite-module leaf
349	  \cref{lem:gf_finite_module}. For \(d \ge 1\), pass to \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\) and
350	  set \(N_K = K \otimes_A N\). If \(N_K = 0\) the torsion base case
351	  \cref{lem:gf_torsion_base} already produces \(f \ne 0\) with \(N_f = 0\) free.
352	  Otherwise let \(m\) be the generic rank of \(N\) over the domain
353	  \(A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\); choosing \(m\) elements of \(N\) whose images form a
354	  \(K(X_1, \dots, X_d)\)-basis of \(N_K\) and clearing denominators gives, after
355	  inverting some \(g \ne 0\) in \(A\), a short exact sequence of
356	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_d]\)-modules
357	  \[
358	    0 \to A_g[X_1, \dots, X_d]^{\oplus m} \to N_g \to T \to 0
359	  \]
360	  with \(T\) a finite torsion \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_d]\)-module. Because \(T\) is
361	  torsion, its support over \(K[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) has dimension \(\le d - 1\), so
362	  \(T\) is finite over a polynomial ring in fewer variables after a further
363	  inversion; the inductive hypothesis yields \(h \ne 0\) with \(T_h\) free over
364	  \(A_{gh}\). The free middle term \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_d]^{\oplus m}\) localises to a
365	  free \(A_{gh}\)-module by the \(d = 0\) leaf applied coordinatewise, and
366	  \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact} splices the two ends of the localised sequence to
367	  give \(N_f\) free over \(A_f\) with \(f := gh\).
368	\end{proof}

370	\subsection{Supporting finite-module helpers}
371	\label{sec:gf_finite_helpers}
372	
373	The following two file-internal helpers in the \texttt{GenericFreeness} namespace package
374	the finite \(A\)-module leaf in the two forms (flatness and module-finiteness) that the
375	chain and the geometric globalization consume. Both are already proved axiom-clean.
376	
377	\begin{lemma}
378	[Flat localization away, finite module]
379	  \label{lem:gf_flat_finite}
380	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_flat_localizationAway_of_finite}
381	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module}
382	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain and \(M\) a finite \(A\)-module. Then there exists
383	  \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that \texttt{LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers f) M} is
384	  \emph{flat} over \texttt{Localization.Away f}.
385	\end{lemma}
386	\begin{proof}
387	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module}
388	  By \cref{lem:gf_finite_module} the localized module is free over
389	  \texttt{Localization.Away f}; free modules are flat (\texttt{Module.Flat.of\_free}).
390	\end{proof}

392	\begin{lemma}
393	[Free localization away, module-finite variant]
394	  \label{lem:gf_free_moduleFinite}
395	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_free_localizationAway_of_moduleFinite}
396	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module}
397	  The module-finiteness packaging of \cref{lem:gf_finite_module}: for \(A\) a noetherian
398	  domain and \(M\) a module that is finite as an \(A\)-module, there exists \(f \ne 0\)
399	  with \texttt{LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers f) M} free over
400	  \texttt{Localization.Away f}.
401	\end{lemma}
402	\begin{proof}
403	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module}
404	  Identical to \cref{lem:gf_finite_module}, packaged against the
405	  \texttt{Module.Finite} hypothesis.
406	\end{proof}

408	
409	\begin{theorem}
410	
411	\leanok
412	[Generic flatness, geometric form]
413	  \label{thm:generic_flatness}
414	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatness}
415	  \uses{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic}
416	  % NOTE (re-signed iter-002): the Lean signature `AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatness`
417	  % previously took `F : X.Modules` with NO coherence hypothesis, under which the
418	  % statement is FALSE (a non-coherent module sheaf need not become flat on any
419	  % non-empty open). It was re-signed in iter-002 (the decl is NOT in
420	  % archon-protected.yaml) to carry the coherence encoding below; the Lean file is
421	  % now the source of truth for the exact binders and the full flatness conclusion.
422	  %
423	  % Mathlib has no single `IsCoherent` predicate at this pin; coherence over a
424	  % locally noetherian base is encoded as the conjunction of two existing
425	  % SheafOfModules predicates on `F`:
426	  %     [F.IsQuasicoherent]   (SheafOfModules.IsQuasicoherent,
427	  %                            Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Sheaf.Quasicoherent)
428	  %     [F.IsFiniteType]      (SheafOfModules.IsFiniteType,
429	  %                            Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Sheaf.Generators)
430	  %
431	  % LEAN SIGNATURE HEADER (now landed in the .lean file):
432	  %   theorem genericFlatness {S X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral S] [IsLocallyNoetherian S]
433	  %       (p : X ⟶ S) [LocallyOfFiniteType p] (F : X.Modules)
434	  %       [F.IsQuasicoherent] [F.IsFiniteType] :
435	  %       ∃ (V : S.Opens), (V : Set S).Nonempty ∧ (the fibrewise flatness conclusion,
436	  %       quantified over affine opens U ≤ V of S and affine opens W ≤ p⁻¹U of X, with
437	  %       Γ(F,W) given its Γ(S,U)-module structure via p.appLE — verbatim form in the
438	  %       .lean file, the source of truth).
439	
440	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``generic flatness''
441	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1781-L1787).
442	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
443	  % "Let \(S\) be a noetherian and integral scheme.
444	  %  Let \(p: X\to S\) be a finite type morphism, and let \(\F\) be a
445	  %  coherent sheaf of \({\mathcal O}_X\)-modules. Then there exists a non-empty
446	  %  open subscheme \(U\subset S\) such that the restriction of \(\F\)
447	  %  to \(X_U = p^{-1}(U)\) is flat over \({\mathcal O}_U\)."
448	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``generic flatness''.}
449	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian integral scheme, \(p : X \to S\) a finite-type morphism,
450	  and \(\F\) a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module. Then there exists a non-empty open
451	  subscheme \(U \subseteq S\) such that the restriction of \(\F\) to
452	  \(X_U = p^{-1}(U)\) is \(\OO_U\)-flat.
453	
454	  The coherence hypothesis on \(\F\) is essential: without it the conclusion is
455	  false, since a non-coherent \(\OO_X\)-module need not become flat over any
456	  non-empty open of \(S\). The intended formal target therefore carries \(\F\)
457	  coherent over the locally noetherian base \(S\), which — absent a single
458	  \texttt{IsCoherent} predicate in Mathlib at this pin — is encoded as the
459	  conjunction of the two module-sheaf hypotheses \(\F\) quasi-coherent
460	  (\texttt{SheafOfModules.IsQuasicoherent}) and \(\F\) of finite type
461	  (\texttt{SheafOfModules.IsFiniteType}), together with \(p\) locally of finite type
462	  and \(S\) integral and locally noetherian.
463	\end{theorem}
464	
465	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "The above theorem has the following consequence, which
466	% follows by restricting attention to a non-empty affine open subscheme of \(S\)."
467	% (Source: nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1776-L1777. The
468	% Nitsure proof is the single sentence quoted: deduce the geometric form from
469	% the algebraic generic flatness statement (\cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic})
470	% by restricting to a non-empty
471	% affine open of \(S\), where the situation reduces to the algebraic statement.)
472	\begin{proof}
473	  
474	  Restrict to a non-empty affine open \(U_0 = \Spec A \subseteq S\), where \(A\) is a
475	  noetherian domain (\(S\) integral and locally noetherian; existence of such a \(U_0\)
476	  by \texttt{exists\_isAffineOpen\_mem\_and\_subset} applied at a point of the non-empty
477	  \(S\)). We assemble the witness \(V\) from the algebraic form on a finite affine cover.
478	
479	  \emph{Step 1 — finite affine cover above \(U_0\).} Since \(p\) is locally of finite
480	  type it is in particular quasi-compact, so the open subscheme \(X_{U_0} = p^{-1}(U_0)\)
481	  is quasi-compact; choose a finite affine cover \(\{W_j = \Spec B_j\}_{j}\) of
482	  \(X_{U_0}\) by affine opens of \(X\). Because \(p\) is locally of finite type, each
483	  ring \(B_j = \Gamma(X, W_j)\) is a finite-type \(A\)-algebra.
484	
485	  \emph{Step 2 — read off a finite module on each patch.} On each \(W_j\), quasi-coherence
486	  of \(\F\) (\texttt{SheafOfModules.IsQuasicoherent}) identifies \(\F|_{W_j}\) with the
487	  associated sheaf \(\widetilde{M_j}\) of the section module \(M_j = \Gamma(\F, W_j)\), and
488	  finite type of \(\F\) (\texttt{SheafOfModules.IsFiniteType}) makes \(M_j\) a finite
489	  \(B_j\)-module. Through the scalar tower \(A \to B_j \to M_j\), \(M_j\) is the data fed
490	  to the algebraic form.
491	
492	  \emph{Step 3 — apply the algebraic form and take the common basic open.}
493	  \cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic} applied to \((A, B_j, M_j)\) yields \(f_j \in A\),
494	  \(f_j \ne 0\), with \((M_j)_{f_j}\) free over \(A_{f_j}\). As the cover is finite, set
495	  \(f := \prod_j f_j \ne 0\) (a non-zero element of the domain \(A\)) and
496	  \(V := D(f) \subseteq \Spec A \subseteq S\), a non-empty (basic) open; over \(V\) every
497	  \((M_j)_f\) is free over \(A_f\) (freeness is preserved by the further localization
498	  \(A_{f_j} \to A_f\)).
499	
500	  \emph{Step 4 — freeness \(\Rightarrow\) flatness, checked locally.} On each patch the
501	  localized section module is free, hence flat over \(A_f\) by
502	  \texttt{Module.Flat.of\_free}; the fibrewise flatness conclusion (flatness of
503	  \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) over \(\Gamma(S, U)\) for affine \(U \le V\), \(W \le p^{-1}U\)) then
504	  follows from these local freeness statements by the localize-at-maximal-ideals
505	  flatness criterion \texttt{Module.flat\_of\_isLocalized\_maximal}. This produces the
506	  required \(V\) with \(\F|_{X_V}\) flat over \(\OO_V\).
507	\end{proof}
508	
509	\section{Mathlib status}
510	\label{sec:flatstrat_mathlib_status}
511	
512	Mathlib (master \texttt{b80f227}) provides \texttt{Module.Flat}
513	(\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Flat.Basic}), the module-theoretic notion of
514	flatness applicable affine-locally to the section modules of \(\F\), together
515	with the dévissage and Noether-normalisation infrastructure that the algebraic
516	generic-flatness argument consumes
517	(\texttt{IsNoetherianRing.induction\_on\_isQuotientEquivQuotientPrime},
518	\texttt{Module.FinitePresentation.exists\_free\_localizedModule\_powers},
519	\texttt{exists\_finite\_inj\_algHom\_of\_fg}).  It does \emph{not} yet contain
520	the polynomial-ring core of generic freeness (a finite module over
521	\(A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) becomes free after inverting a single element), nor the
522	geometric statement \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatness}
523	(\cref{thm:generic_flatness}) packaging the algebraic form
524	(\cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic}) over a noetherian integral base.  These
525	are introduced in
526	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FlatteningStratification.lean}; the
527	file-internal \texttt{GenericFreeness} namespace already lands the
528	finite-module / finite-morphism special case axiom-clean.
